But in Java, the code would be:

public int kthFactor(int n, int k) {

    List<Integer> smaller = new ArrayList<>();
    List<Integer> larger = new ArrayList<>();

    for (int i = 1; i <= Math.sqrt(n); i++) {
        if (n % i == 0) {
            smaller.add(i);
            if (i != n / i) {
                larger.add(n / i);
            }
        }
    }

    // Now, create the factors list
    List<Integer> factors = new ArrayList<>(smaller);
    Collections.reverse(larger);
    factors.addAll(larger);

    if (factors.size() < k) {
        return -1;
    } else {
        return factors.get(k-1);
    }
}

solution: 
```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.Collections;
import java.util.List;

class Solution {
    public int kthFactor(int n, int k) {
        List<Integer> smaller = new ArrayList<>();
        List<Integer> larger = new ArrayList<>();
        
        int sqrtN = (int) Math.sqrt(n);
        for (int i = 1; i <= sqrtN; i++) {
            if (n % i == 0) {
                smaller.add(i);
                if (i != n / i) {
                    larger.add(n / i);
                }
            }
        }
        
        List<Integer> factors = new ArrayList<>(smaller);
        Collections.reverse(larger);
        factors.addAll(larger);
        
        if (factors.size() < k) {
            return -1;
        } else {
            return factors.get(k - 1);
        }
    }
}
```

efficiency: HIGH

time complexity: O(√n)

space complexity: O(√n)

energy implications: LOW

explanation: This solution efficiently finds the k-th factor by leveraging the properties of factor pairs, reducing the time complexity to O(√n). It collects factors in two lists, smaller and larger, then combines them to form the complete list of factors in ascending order. This approach ensures optimal performance and minimal energy usage.
